questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>298</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>, the equilibrium constant of the process <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow></msub><mo>⇌</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>29</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Standard free energy change (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kJ</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> ) of the process is approximately <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>314</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>;</mo><mo> </mo><mi>log</mi><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>47</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
questions.solution:
Free energy change of a reaction is calculated by,

∆G=∆G∘+RT lnQ

At equilibrium, the free energy change of a reaction is zero (∆G=0), and the reaction quotient (Q) is equal to the equilibrium constant (K). So, the above relation takes the form,

∆G∘=-RT lnK

∆G∘=-2.303RT log10K

where ∆G∘=standard free energy change

Therefore,

∆G∘=-2.303×8.314×298×log3×10-29 J mol-1

∆G∘=-5705.8×log3+log10-29 J mol-1

∆G∘=-5705.8×0.47-29 J mol-1

∆G∘=162787J mol-1

∆G∘≈163 kJ mol-1
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Calculate enthalpy for formation of ethylene from the following data
(I) $\mathrm{C}_{\text {(graphite) }}+\mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})$
$\Delta H=393.5 \mathrm{~kJ}$
(II) $\mathrm{H}_2(g)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(g) \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(l)$;
$\Delta U=-256.2 \mathrm{~kJ}$
(III) $\begin{array}{r}\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_4(g)+3 \mathrm{O}_2(g) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_2(g)+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(l) ; \\ \Delta H=-1410.8 \mathrm{~kJ}\end{array}$
questions.solution:
(I) $\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_2 ; \quad \Delta H=-393.5 \mathrm{~kJ}$
(II) $\mathrm{H}_2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} ; \quad \Delta H=-286.2 \mathrm{~kJ}$
(III) $\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_4+3 \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_2+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$;
$\Delta H=-1410.8 \mathrm{~kJ}$
Multiply the equations (I) and (II) by 2
(IV) $2 \mathrm{C}+2 \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_2 ; \Delta H=-787.0 \mathrm{~kJ}$
(V) $2 \mathrm{H}_2+\mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} ; \Delta H=-572.4 \mathrm{~kJ}$
Add the equations (IV) and (V) and then subtract (III).
$2 \mathrm{C}+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_4 ; \quad \Delta H=51.4 \mathrm{~kJ}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Enthalpy of sublimation of iodine is <math><mn>24</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at <math><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>200</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> . If specific heat of <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> (s) and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> (vap) are <math><mn>0.055</mn></math> and <math><mn>0.031</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively, then enthalpy of sublimation of iodine at <math><mn>250</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> in <math><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is:
questions.solution:
I2s→I2g
ΔH250oC-ΔH200oC=ΔCpmT2-T1
ΔH250oC-24=0.013-0.055×50
ΔH250oC-24=-1.2
(ΔH)250oC=22.8 J/gm
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.q: The heats of combustion of <math> <mrow> <msub> <mtext>C</mtext> <mi>x</mi> </msub> <msub> <mtext>H</mtext> <mi>y</mi> </msub> <mtext>, </mtext> </mrow></math>carbon and hydrogen are a, b and c cal respectively. The heat of formation of <math> <mrow><msub><mtext>C</mtext> <mi>x</mi> </msub> <msub> <mtext>H</mtext> <mi>y</mi> </msub> <mtext>,</mtext> </mrow> </math> will be:
questions.solution:
xC +  y 2 H 2 → C x H y            ΔH f CxHy  = ?
ΔH f CxHy  =  ΔH rx n
= ΔH comb . CxHy − xΔH comb . C − y 2 ΔH comb . H 2
= a−x b − y 2 c
= − xb +  yc 2 −a  cal

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>92</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><mi>Na</mi></math> reacts with water in open vessel at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. What is the value of work done? [Assume ideal nature of the gaseous product]
questions.solution:
Reaction of sodium with water will be 

2Na(s) + 2 H2O(l) → 2NaOH(aq) + H2(g)

According to the given reaction 46 g of Na gives one mole of hydrogen gas.

So, 92 g of Na will give =9246 = 2 moles of H2 gas

This is the only gaseous product so, due to this volume will increase at constant pressure.

Work done at constant pressure and temperature

w=-P∆V = -∆ngRT  since product is ideal gas.

w=-2×8.314×300 J = -4988.4 J

Negative sign indicate that work is done by the system.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: For the reaction, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mtext>(rhombic) </mtext></msub><mo>+</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>→</mo><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ΔH</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>298</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>  at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>atm</mi></math>. Therefore, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Δ</mi><mi>E</mi></math> for the reaction should be
questions.solution:
Given : ΔH=-298 kJ mol-1

T=25+273=298 K,P=1 atm,ΔE=?

ΔH=ΔE+ΔngRT

or ΔE=ΔH ∵Δng=0

ΔE=-298

ΔE=-298 kJ mol-1
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The standard enthalpy of formation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mo>∆</mo><msubsup><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mo>∘</mo></msubsup><mo>)</mo></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>298 </mn><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> for methane, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CH</mi><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-74.5 </mo><mi>kJ</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>mol</mi></math>. The additional information required to determine the average energy for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> bond dissociation would be ________.
options: the dissociation energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">2</mi></msub></math> and enthalpy of sublimation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>
latent heat of vaporization of methane
the first four ionisation energies of carbon and electron gain enthalpy of hydrogen
questions.solution:
Chemical equation corresponding to formation of methane is :

C(graphite)+2H2 →CH4(g)  ∆Hf∘=-74.5 kJ/mol

Enthalpy change of any reaction can be written in terms of bond energy:

∆H=∑Bond energy of reactants-∑Bond energy of products

∆Hf∘=(Sublimation energy of graphite+2 (B.D.E)H-H)-(4×(B.D.E.)C-H)B.D.E.→Bond Dissociation Energy

Enthalpy of sublimation of graphite and bond dissociation energy of H-H is not given in question.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
What is the standard enthalpy of reaction (in kJ) when two moles of $\mathrm{Fe}_2 \mathrm{O}_3(s)$ reacts with $\mathrm{H}_2$ gas to give Fe metal?
$\Delta H_f^{\circ}$ of $\mathrm{Fe}_2 \mathrm{O}_3(s)$ and $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(l)$ are -824.2 and $-285.83 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively.
questions.solution:
When $\mathrm{Fe}_2 \mathrm{O}_3(s)$ reacts with $\mathrm{H}_2(g)$, it will give $\mathrm{Fe}$
and $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ as follows: $\mathrm{Fe}_2 \mathrm{O}_3+3 \mathrm{H}_2 \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}+3 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ When 2 moles of $\mathrm{Fe}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ are used, the reaction becomes as follows:
$2\left[\mathrm{Fe}_2 \mathrm{O}_3+3 \mathrm{H}_2 \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}+3 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right]$
Given, $\Delta H_f^{\circ}$ for $\mathrm{Fe}_2 \mathrm{O}_3(s)=-824.2 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and $\Delta H_f^{\circ}$ for $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(l)=-285.83 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
$\because$ Standard enthalpy of reaction
$\left(\Delta H_R^{\circ}\right)=\sum \Delta H_f^{\circ}$ (Product) $-\Sigma \Delta H_f^{\circ}$ (Reacant $)$
and $\left.\begin{array}{c}H_f^{\circ} \text { for } \mathrm{H}_2=0 \\ H_f^{\circ} \text { for } \mathrm{Fe}=0\end{array}\right]$ (as are present in free state)
Thus,
$\begin{aligned} \Delta H_R^{\circ} & =[6 \times-285.83]-[2 \times(-824.2)] \\ \Delta H_R^{\circ} & =(-1714.98)+(1648.4) \\ \Delta H_R^{\circ} & =-66.58 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}\end{aligned}$
Hence, option (a) is the correct answer.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Consider the reversible isothermal expansion of an ideal gas in a closed system at two different temperatures <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced><mrow><mo> </mo><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo><</mo><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> The correct graphical depiction of the dependence of work done <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>w</mi></mfenced></math> on the final volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>v</mi></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math>
questions.solution:
Let the gas is expanded from V1 to V at T1 and from V2 to Vat T2

∴ At T1

W1=nRT1lnVV1=nRTℓnV-ℓnV1

Similarly at T2

W2=nRT2lnV-lnV2)

∴W1=nRT1lnV-nRT1lnV1

W2=nRT2lnV-nRT2lnV2

Slope of W2>Slope of W1

As nRT2>nRT1 T2>T1

∴ The intercept of W2 is more negative than that of W1 because V2>V1.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Match the following.
The correct answer is $\begin{array}{llll} A & B & C & D \end{array}$
questions.solution:
(A) At constant volume the change in internal energy of a system is calculated by $q V=\Delta U$
(B) Isothermal irreversible change is
$$
W=-p_{\text {ex }}\left(V_f-V_i\right)
$$
(C) Isothermal reversible change is
$$
W=-2.303 n R T \log \frac{V_t}{V_i}
$$
(D) Adiabatic change $W_{\text {adiabatic }}=\Delta U$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: \(6 \mathrm{~g}\) of graphite is burnt in a bomb calorimeter at \(25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\) and \(1 \mathrm{~atm}\) pressure. The temperature of water increased from \(25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\) to \(31^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\). If \(\Delta H\) of this reaction is \(-248 \mathrm{~kJ}\) mol, find out \(\mathrm{C}_V\) (in \(\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{K}^{-1}\) ) of bomb calorimeter.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The heat required to rise the temperature of $54 \mathrm{~g}$ of aluminium from $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in $\mathrm{J}$ is (molar heat capacity of aluminium in this temperature range is $24 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$; atomic weight of $\mathrm{Al}$ is 27)
questions.solution:
Given,

Weight of aluminium $(w)=54 \mathrm{~g}$
Temperature difference $(\Delta T)=60^{\circ}-40^{\circ}=20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
Molar heat capacity of aluminium
$$
\left(C_s\right)=24 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}
$$

Atomic mass of $(\mathrm{Al})$, i.e. $(M)=27$
$\because \operatorname{Moles}$ of $(\mathrm{Al})$, i.e. $n=\frac{w}{M}=\frac{54}{27}=2 \mathrm{~mol}$
Heat required $(Q)=n C_s \Delta T$
$$
=2 \times 24 \times 20=960 \mathrm{~J}
$$

Hence, option (c) is the correct answer.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: For the reaction, $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_2(g)$, if dinitrogen tetroxide is $50 \%$ dissociated at $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the standard free energy change at this temperature and $1 \mathrm{~atm}$ pressure is
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The change in the internal energy when a system goes from state A to B is 40 kJ mole-1. If the system follows a reversible path from A to B and returns to state A by an irreversible path, what would be the net change in internal energy?
questions.solution:
We know that, internal energy is a state function, hence it only depends upon the initial and final values irrespective of the path followed,

i.e., ∆U=Ufinal-Uinitial

For the given cyclic process:

Ufinal=Uinitial

Thus, the net change in internal energy change is zero.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Which of the following substances has highest value of standard molar enthalpy of formation at \(298 \mathrm{~K}\) ?
questions.solution:
Higher the stability of a substance, higher will be its standard molar enthalpy of formation value, \(\left|\Delta_f H^{\circ}\right|\)
Among the halides, \(\mathrm{F}^{-}\)is smallest and \(\mathrm{I}^{-}\)is largest in size. So, according to Fajan's rule, \(\mathrm{NaF}\) is mostly ionic in nature.
\(\mathrm{NaI}  < \mathrm{NaBr} < \mathrm{NaCl} < \mathrm{NaF}\)
\(\longrightarrow\) Ionic nature
\(\longrightarrow\) Thermal stability
So, \(\mathrm{NaF}\) has highest value of \(\left|\Delta_f H^{\circ}\right|\).
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Standard entropies of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>XY</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>40</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>K</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>mol</mi></math> respectively. At what temperature the following reaction will be at equilibrium
questions.solution:
So from this question we know that enthalpy for the reaction is -30 KJ

12X2 + 32Y2 → XY3           ∆H = -30 KJ

So we know from the concept of standard entropies is 

∆S = ∆SP - ∆SR∆S = ∆SXY3 -32 ∆SY2-12∆SX2∆S = 50 - 32×40-12×60∆S = -40 JK-1mol-1

So according to the second law of thermodynamics

∆G = ∆H-T∆S

For equilibrium the free Gibb's energy is ∆G = 0

∆H = T∆ST = ∆H∆ST = -30×103J-40JK-1

So the value of temperature is 

T = 750 K
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, dinitrogen tetroxide is fifty percent dissociated. Find its standard free energy change at this temperature and one atmosphere. [ Given: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>log</mi><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>33</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1239</mn></math> ]
questions.solution:
The reaction showing the dissociation will be:

N2O4(s)⇌2NO2(g)t=01 mol0 molt=teq0.5 mol1 mol

Clearly, from this equation we can say that the total number of moles is 

0.5+1=1.5 mol

Hence, the partial pressure for the molecule will be

PN2O4 = 0.51.5×1atm = 13 atmPNO2 = 11.5×1atm =23 atm

Now, according to the law of chemical equilibrium we know that 

Ksp = P2NO2PN2O4 = 23213 = 1.33 atm

We know that at equilibrium the free Gibb's energy is: 

∆G = -2.303×RT×log10 KP∆G = -2.303×8.314 JK-1mol-1×333K×log10 1.33∆G = -790 Jmol-1
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: One gram-atom of graphite and one gram-atom of diamond were separately burnt to form <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> . The amount of heat liberated was 393.5KJ and 305.4KJ respectively. Then, which of the following is the correct about diamond and graphite?
options: Graphite has greater affinity for oxygen.
Diamond has greater affinity for oxygen.
Graphite is more stable than diamond.
Product obtained from diamond  is more stable than that from graphite.
questions.solution:
More the energy released, more is the thermodynamic stability of the species.

Hence, graphite is more stable than diamond.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: What is the amount of work done when 0.5 mole of methane, <math><mi>C</mi><msub><mrow><mi>H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>g</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is subjected to combustion at 300 K? (Given, <math><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8.314</mn><mi> </mi><mi>J</mi><mi> </mi><msup><mrow><mi>K</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><mi>o</mi><msup><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> )
questions.solution:
C H 4 ( g )+2 O 2 ( g )→C O 2 ( g )+2 H 2 O( l )
Amount of work done =-∆nRT
1 mole of methane reacts with 2 moles of oxygen to produce 2 moles of CO2
Hence, 0.5 mole of CH4 reacts with 1 mole of O2 to produce 1 mole of CO2.
∴  n1= moles of reactants = 1.5
n2= moles of product = 1
∴   ∆n=n2-n1=1-1.5=-0.5
w=--0.5×300×8.314 JK-1 mol-1
=+2494 J
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Mark out the enthalpy of formation of carbon monoxide (CO).

Given, Cs+O2g→CO2g, ∆H=-393.3kJ/mol and COg+12O2g→CO2g; ΔH=-282.2 kJ/mol.
questions.solution:
To find:Cs+12O2g → COg=0;    ΔHf∘=??        (I)

Given:

Cs+O2g→CO2g;     ΔHf∘1=-393.3 kJ/mol       IICOs+12O2g→CO2g ;   ΔHf∘2=-282.2 kJ/mol      III

Equation (I) can be founded by subtracting equation (III) from (II),

So,  ΔHf∘ = ΔHf∘1 - ΔHf∘2ΔHf∘=-393.3 - -282.2ΔHf∘ = -111.1 KJ/mol-1
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A gas absorbs $200 \mathrm{~J}$ heat and expands by $500 \mathrm{~cm}^3$ against a constant external pressure $2 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$. What is the change in internal energy?
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \Delta V=500 \mathrm{~cm}^3=0.5 \mathrm{dm}^3 \\ & \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{ext}}=2 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}=2 \text { bar } \\ & \text { (since, } 1 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}=1 \text { bar) } \\ & \mathrm{W}=-\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{ext}} \times \Delta \mathrm{V} \\ & =-2 \times(0.5) \\ & =-1 \mathrm{dm}^3 \text { bar } \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{W}=-100 \mathrm{~J} \quad\left(\text { since, } 1 \mathrm{dm}^3 \mathrm{bar}=100 \mathrm{~J}\right) \\ & \text { According to first law of thermodynamics, } \\ & \Delta U=Q+W=200 \mathrm{~J}-100 \mathrm{~J}=+100 \mathrm{~J} \\ & \end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
The formation of the oxide ion O2-(g) requires first, an exothermic and then, an endothermic step as shown below:

Og+e-=O-g;  ∆Ho=-142 kJ mol-1

O-g+e-=O2-g, ∆Ho=844 kJ mol-1. This is because
options: <math><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> ion will tend to resist the addition of another electron.
<math><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> ion has comparatively larger size than oxygen atom.
questions.solution: The addition of second electron to an atom or ion is always endothermic as the incoming electron experience a greater force of repulsion from the other electrons.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
A thermodynamic cycle in the pressure P-volume V plane is given below:

AB and CD are isothermal processes while BC and DA are adiabatic processes. The same cycle in the temperature T- entropy S plane is :
questions.solution:
AB is isothermal reversible expansion process i.e. ΔT=0 and S increases as there is increase in volume.

BC is adiabatic reversible expansion process qrev=0 i.e. temperature decreases and ΔS=0

CD is isothermal reversible compression process i.e. ΔT=0 and S decreases as there is decrease in volume.

DA is adiabatic reversible compression process qres=0 i.e. temperature decreases and ΔS=0
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The enthalpies of combustion of C(graphite) and C(diamond) are -393.8 and $-395.3 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively. The enthalpy of conversion of $\mathrm{C}$ (graphite) to $\mathrm{C}$ (diamond) is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{eq}^{\mathrm{n}} 1 \ldots . . \mathrm{C}(\text { graphite })+\mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \quad \Delta \mathrm{H}_1=-393.8 \frac{\mathrm{KJ}}{\mathrm{mol}} \\ & \mathrm{eq}^{\mathrm{n}} 2 \ldots . . \mathrm{C}(\text { diamond })+\mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \quad \Delta \mathrm{H}_2=-395.3 \frac{\mathrm{KJ}}{\mathrm{mol}} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{eq}^{\mathrm{n}} 1-\mathrm{eq}^{\mathrm{n} 2}=\Delta \mathrm{H}_1-\Delta \mathrm{H}_2 \\ & =-393.8-(-395.3) \\ & =1.5 \frac{\mathrm{KJ}}{\mathrm{mol}}\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: q, w, ΔE and ΔH for the following process ABCD for a monoatomic gas are:
questions.solution:
Temperature at ATA=PV0nR

TB=2P0V0nR

Since BC curve isothermal

∴TC=2P0V0nR

TD=P0/2·2V0nR=P0V0nR=TA

Now

∵TD-TA=0

∴ΔU=ΔH=0

∵ For ideal gas ΔU=nCVΔTΔH=nCPΔT

WT=WAB+WBC+WCD

=-P0V0+-nRTln4V02V0+-P02×-2V0

Wτ=-2P0V0ln2 and q=-WT
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Given:

C graphite + O 2 g → CO 2 g  ; Δ r H o =−393.5  kJ mol −1

H2g+12O2g→H2Ol;ΔrHo=-285.8 kJ mol-1

CO2g+2H2Ol→CH4g+2O2g;ΔrHo=+890.3 kJ mol-1

Based on the above thermochemical equations, the value of ΔrHo at 298 K for the reaction

Cgraphite+2H2g→CH4g will be:
questions.solution:
For reaction

CO 2 (g)+2H 2 O(l)→ CH 4 (g)+2O 2 (g)

ΔrHo=ΣΔfHoproducts-ΣΔfHoreactants

=ΔrHoCH4+2×0-ΔfHoCO2+2ΔfHoH2O

+890.3=ΔfHoCH4--393.5+2×-285.8

Δ f H o of CH 4  (g)=−74.8 kJ/mol
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The molar heat capacity of water at constant pressure P, is <math><mn>75 </mn><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . When 1.0 kJ of heat is supplied to 100 g of water which is free to expand, the increase in temperature of water is
questions.solution:
q=n.CpΔT
1000=10018×75×ΔT
So ΔT=2.4 K.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The enthalpy of neutralization of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mi>OH</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mi>COOH</mi><mo> </mo></math>is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kcal</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>​ and enthalpy of neutralisation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mi>OH</mi><mo> </mo></math>with a strong acid is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>11</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kcal</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The enthalpy of ionization of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mi>COOH</mi></math> will be
questions.solution:
Enthalpy of neutralisation is the amount of energy released during the combination of one moe strong monobasic acid neutralised with the one-mole strong monoacidic base.

H+aq + OH-aq→H2Ol  ∆neut.H =-13.7 kcal

Enthalpy of ionisation of CH3COOH & NH4OH together

= - 10.5 - - 13.7 = + 3.2 Kcal

Enthalpy of ionisation of only NH4OH

=-11.7--13.7=+2 Kcal

Enthalpy of ionisation of only CH3COOH

=3.2-2=1.2 Kcal
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A solution of 200 mL of 1 M KOH is added to 200 mL of 1 M HCl and the mixture is well shaken. This rise in temperature <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> is noted. The experiment is repeated by using 100 mL of each solution and increase in temperature <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> is again noted. Which of the following is correct?
options: <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> is four times as large as <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math>
questions.solution:
Heat produced by 200 Meq. of KOH + HCl
=13.7×2001000kcal
This is used to raise the temperature of 400 mL. Solution say T1
Thus, 13.7×2001000=400×S×T1
Now, heat produced by 100 Meq of KOH + HCl
=13.7×1001000
Thus, -13.7×1001000=200×S×T2
It is evident that T1=T2 .
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Calculate Δ f G ∘ for (NH4Cl, s) at 310 K.

Given : Δ f H ∘ NH 4 Cl, s = - 314.5 kJ/mol ; Δ r C p = 0

S N 2 g ∘ = 192 JK -1 mol -1 ;   S H 2 g ∘ = 130.5 JK -1 mol -1 ;

S Cl 2 g ∘ = 233 JK -1 mol -1 ;   S NH 4 Cl s ∘ = 99.5 JK -1 mol -1

All given data at 300 K.
questions.solution:
∆fS0NH4Cl,s at 300 K

= S N H 4 Cl(S) 0 −[ 1 2 S N 2 0 +2 S H 2 0 + 1 2 S C l 2 0 ]

= -374 JK-1mol-1  

∴ ∆fCp=0

∴  ∆fS3100=∆rS3000

= -374 JK-1mol-1

∆fH3100=∆fH3000=-314.5

∆fG3100=∆fH0-310∆fS0

= -314.5-310-3741000

-198.56 kJ/mol
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Calculate the final temperature of the gas, if one mole of an ideal gas is allowed to expand reversibly and adibatically from a temperature of 27°C and the work done during the process is 3 kJ .
Given C V  = 20 J/K
questions.solution:
Given number of moles =1
Initial temperature = 27 o C=300 K
Work done by the system =3 kJ=3000 K
It will be (-) because work is done by the system.
Heat capacity at constant volume Cv  = 20 J/k
We know that work done
W=-nCV(T2-T1) ;
3000=-1×20(T2-300)
3000=-20T2+6000
20 T 2 =3000 T 2 = 3000 20 =150 K
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: For vaporization of water at 1 atmospheric pressure, the values of <math><mo>∆H</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>∆S</mo></math> are 40.63 <math><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>108.8</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively. The temperature when Gibbs energy change <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>∆</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">G</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> for this transformation will be zero, is:
questions.solution:
H 2 O( l ) ⇌ 1 atm H 2 O( g )

∆H=40630 J mol-1

∆S=108.8 JK-1 mol-1

∆G=∆H-T∆S When ∆G=0,

∆H-T ∆ S=0

T= ΔH ΔS = 40630 J mol  K −1 108.8 J mol  K −1 =373.4 K.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The given p-V diagram shows gases during adiabatic process. Plots 1 and 2 should correspond respectively to
questions.solution:
We know that

dpdV= -γ pV

∴      Slope ∝γ

From the fig. (i), slope of 2 > slope of 1.So, plots 1 and 2 should correspond to O2 and He respectively.
[ γ He = 5 3  ,  γ 0 2 = 7 5 ]
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: An ideal gas on isothermal reversible compression from $10 \mathrm{~L}$ to $5 \mathrm{~L}$ performs $1730 \mathrm{~J}$ of work at $300 \mathrm{~K}$. Calculate number of moles of gas involved in compression? $\left(\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{V}_1=10 \mathrm{~L}, \mathrm{~V}_2=5 \mathrm{~L}, \mathrm{~W}=1730 \mathrm{~J}, \mathrm{~T}=300 \mathrm{~K}, \mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}, \mathrm{n} \\
& =? \\
& \mathrm{~W}_{\max }=-2.303 \mathrm{nRT} \log _{10} \frac{\mathrm{V}_2}{\mathrm{~V}_1} \\
& \therefore 1730 \mathrm{~J}=-2.303 \mathrm{n} \times 8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \times 300 \mathrm{~K} \times \log _{10}\left(\frac{5}{10}\right) \\
& \therefore \mathrm{n}=\frac{1730 \mathrm{~J}}{-2.303 \times 8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{mo}^{-1} \times 300 \mathrm{~K} \times \log _{10}(0.5)} \\
& \therefore \mathrm{n}=\frac{1730}{-5744.14 \times(-0.3010)} \mathrm{mol} \\
& \therefore \mathrm{n}=\frac{1730}{1729} \approx 1 \mathrm{~mol}
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
If ∆Hfo for H2O2 and H2O are -188 kJ/mol and -286 kJ/mol, what will be the enthalpy change of the reaction:

2H2O2l→2H2Ol+O2(g)
questions.solution:
Given reaction is 2H2O2l→2H2Ol+O2(g)

ΔH Reaction o = ΔH f o Products − ΔH f o Reactants

Enthalpy change =2×-286-2×-188

=-196 kJ
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Two gases are in contact with each other so heat can flow between them. Assume no heat loss to surrounding. One gas is hot and the other gas is cold initially. Identify what happened to gases after the equilibrium condition?
options: The particles in the cold gas will have slowed in their rate of movement.
The particles in the hot gas will have increased their rate of movement.
The temperature of the cold gas will have decreased
The temperature of the hot gas will have decreased
questions.solution: In order to achieve equilibrium, heat from hotter gas starts flowing to the cold gas until their temperatures become the same. Therefore, due to the heat loss temperature of hot gas will have decreased.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Given that <math><mi>d</mi><mi>E</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>T</mi><mi>d</mi><mi>S</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>p</mi><mi>d</mi><mi>V</mi><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi> </mi><mi>H</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>E</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>p</mi><mi>V</mi></math>. Which one of the following relations is true?
questions.solution:
Given, dE=TdS-pdV ...(i)

H=E+pV ...(ii)

Differentiating Eq. (ii),

dH=dE+d(Vp)dH=dE+pdV+Vdp ...(iii)
Put the value of dE
From Eqs. (i) and (iii), we get

dH=(TdS-pdV)+pdV+VdpdH=TdS+Vdp
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> moles of ideal gas expand isothermally and reversibly from a pressure of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>atm</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>atm</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. What is the largest mass which can be lifted through a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> meter?
questions.solution:
Work done, w=-nRTlnP1P2=-10×8.314×300ln101=-57441.42 J

w=-mgh

∴m×9.81×100=57441.42⇒ m=58.55 kg
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: An adiabatic vessel contains n<sub>1</sub> = 3 mole of diatomic gas. Moment of inertia of each molecule is I = 2.76 x 10<sup>-46</sup> kg m<sup>2</sup> and root-mean-square angular velocity is <math> <mrow> <msub> <mi>ω</mi> <mn>0</mn> </msub> <mo>=</mo> <mn>5</mn> <mo>×</mo> <mn>1</mn> <msup> <mn>0</mn> <mrow> <mn>1</mn> <mn>2</mn> </mrow> </msup> <mo>rad/s</mo> </mrow> </math>. Another adiabatic vessel contains n<sub>2</sub> = 5 mole of a monatomic gas at a temperature 470 K. Assume gases to be ideal, calculate root-mean-square angular velocity of diatomic molecules when the two vessel are connected by a thin tube of negligible volume. Boltzmann constant k = 1.38 x 10<sup>-23</sup> J/molecule.
questions.solution:
We know according to law of equipartition of energy, each gas molecule has 1/2 kT energy associated with each of its degrees of freedom. As a diatomic gas molecule has two rotational degrees of freedom, final temperature of the system is

             T f⁡ = f⁡ 1 n 1 T 1 + f⁡ 2 n 2 T 2 f⁡ 1 n 1 + f⁡ 2 n 2                     ...(i)

Here for diatomic gas,

     f⁡ 1 = 5 ,  n = 3  and  T1 = 250 K

For monatomic gas,

   f⁡ 2 = 3 ,  n2 = 5  and  T2 = 470 K

Thus from Eq. (i),

       T f⁡ = 5 × 3 × 2 5 0 + 3 × 5 × 4 7 0 5 × 3 + 3 × 5 = 3 6 0 K

Thus final mixture of the two gases is at temperature 360 K. If final rms angular velocity of diatomic gas molecules is ω rms f⁡ , according to law of equipartition of energy, we have

      1 2 I ω rms f⁡ 2 = kT

or       ω rms f⁡ = 2 kT I

                    = 2 × 1.38 × 1 0 - 2 3 × 3 6 0 2.76 × 1 0 - 4 6

           ω rms f⁡ = 6 × 1 0 1 2 rad/s
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A rigid and insulated tank of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> volume is divided into two compartments. One compartment of volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> contains an ideal gas at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8314</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MPa</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> and while the second compartment of volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> contains the same gas at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>314</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MPa</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. If the partition between the two compartments is ruptured, the final temperature of the gas is
questions.solution:
Moles of the gas in the first compartment:

n1=P1V1RT1=0.8314×106×28.314×400=500

Similarly, n2=2000

The tank is a rigid and insulated, hence, w=0 and q=0. Therefore, ΔU=0.

Let Tf and Pf denote the final temperature and pressure, respectively.

ΔU=n1CV, mTf-T1+n2CV, mTf-T2=0
500Tf-400+2000Tf-500=0
T f = 480 K
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
